learning_outcomes.description: Plan and manage a small-scale social research project
Conduct data collection using methods relevant to your research questions and aims
Understand and address ethical issues relating to social research as they arise in the context of conducting research
Analyse, synthesise and present empirical research data to mount a convincing argument
Write research reports and outputs for different audiences
Reflect critically upon your learning and your achievements
Transfer your knowledge and skills to diverse professional employment contexts

assessments.assessment_title: Research Project Reflection and Methods Summary Exam
assessments.due_date:
20/08/2026 1:00 pm

To be completed in-person, in class on Week 4.
assessments.task_description:
This assessment is an in-class exam that will include 2 parts: a Research Project Reflection and a Methods Summary. This is an individual assessment, and will be undertaken in the Week 4 seminar. This exam is worth 30%, and will be completed in class without the aid of digital devices or pre-written/printed material. 90 minutes will be provided.


Part 1: Reflection (15% of 30%)

For this part of the exam, you will need to complete an in-class reflection (approx. 200-400 words) that captures a significant learning experience in the research project course. Your reflections will follow the three phases of a reflective model known as the “What” Reflection model (Driscoll, 2000). The model, and some useful prompt questions to help steer your thinking, are included below.

BACKGROUND TO THIS ASSESSMENT:

This is intended to help you take stock, capture your learning and help you focus your attention on the semester ahead. This task has been developed because the skills of observation and reflection are critical to research. Being a good researcher means constantly reflecting on your practice and seeking ways to continually develop your skills. Doing something well doesn’t mean much if you don’t know why it worked and how you can replicate it. Doing something poorly isn’t a disaster if it provides a rich learning opportunity and leads to greater insight and improvements for next time. In putting your reflection together you are being invited to hold your work both appreciatively and critically. Neither false modesty nor an over-inflated ego will be helpful to this exercise.

The reflection can be related to either:

  • an interpersonal learning experience (e.g., a disagreement within the group),

or

  • a practical work-based learning experience (e.g., challenges writing or communicating in your ethics application).


This assessment is worth 15%, and will be completed in class without the aid of digital devices or pre-written/printed material. The reflection will follow the structure below. 90 minutes will be provided.

Title of reflection:

1. What?  

This is the descriptive phase of your reflection that captures what happened and what the impact on you was. What you need to do is to reflect on your experiences in the Research Courses (SOSC3201/SOSC3202) so far and choose one important learning moment for you.

For example, it might have been:

  • insight about co-writing;
  • a disagreement within the group;
  • inability to find time to meet with your industry partner;
  • challenges in working out how to project management tasks;
  • etc.

Include a brief description of the event, situation or issue. State your reaction to the situation or issue.

  • Describe the experience - what happened?
  • What did you observe/feel/question about the situation/issue?
  • What was the impact on you?

2. So what?  

This is the analytical phase of your reflection that captures your deeper insights. So, what sense do you make of this? So what have you learnt from this? Now what other ideas do you connect to? (eg from coursework, current events, theories, past experiences).

3. Now what?

This is the intention phase of the reflection that shows how you will apply what you have learned from an experience, to future endeavours.

Now what are you going to do as a result of your experience? Now what do you need to take with you from this experience? Now what will you do to apply what you have learnt in the future? Now what are the critical questions to further pursue? Now what thinking is required? Now what actions are needed?


Part 2: Methods Summary (15% of 30%)

As your research project develops, you should be able to explain to other researchers how you performed your study, and the effects your methods have on the validity of the findings. For this, you are to provide a written summary of your project's methods (approx. 200-400 words). This summary should communicate content clearly and appropriately for an academic audience. This summary should include a brief overview of your methods at this point in your project, and any strengths, limitations, and areas for future direction you have noted. Further details and marking crieria will be provided in class.


Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This exam will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.


Driscoll, J. (2000). Reflective Practice for Learning and Professional Development. In J. Driscoll (Ed.), Practising Clinical Supervision: A Reflective Approach for Healthcare Professionals (pp. 47-61). Baillière Tindall.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The exam will occur in person on Thursday, 20th August, from 11AM in class. This exam will be completed by hand, in-person/in class . Please provide your hard copy worksheet to the course coordinator or teaching assistant, along with your student ID to confirm identity.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.due_date:
25/09/2026 2:00 pm

Peer Assessment Factor: Each individual will need to submit a peer assessment via BuddyCheck on Blackboard, covering the Summary Research Findings within a week of completing this assessment.
assessments.task_description:
As a group, you are required to write a single A4 (double-sided) summary research findings sheet for your research participants and other relevant stakeholders. How you present it is up to you, but consider ways of making it look eye-catching, easily digestible and something that someone will actually stop to read. The sheet should contain the following material:

  • A clear statement of the research problem, aims, questions, and methods
  • A discussion of your emerging research findings, structured by theme.


You should find ways of presenting your data in a suitable format, such as through quotes, tables, graphs. While your document should not be heavily referenced, you should highlight a few key references that would be relevant for participants.

The Summary should also contain details of how the research team might be contacted, and some form of acknowledgement of your research participants.

Examples will be provided to you in class and on Blackboard to illustrate what is required.

Feedback on your Summary will be provided and you are expected to revise the Summary before providing to the industry partner and circulating to your participants. You are likely to need the industry partner's permission (e.g. for logo) before you can make your Summary available to participants and may need their assistance with distribution. Please do not send this to participants without giving them an opportunity to give feedback.

The length of this document should be no more than two sides of one A4 sheet. 

AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students are required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.

 Marking criteria and matrix are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course.

This piece of assessment is designed to meet three aims.

  1. As part of ethically-informed research, it is important that other researchers, research participants and other relevant stakeholders are given access to the research findings in one form or another. While it is your industry partner who has effectively 'contracted' the research, the study would not have been possible without your research participants and it is important that you 'give something back' to acknowledge their time and effort.
  2. This assignment represents a first opportunity for you to start writing up your research findings and to consider what your main themes and argument are in preparation for your video (assessment 3), and research report (assessment 4).
  3. This assignment is designed to equip you with the skills of writing to different audiences, that is communicable and easy to understand, rather than using technical or academic language or 'jargon'.
assessments.submission_guidelines: As a group assessment, only one member of the team will need to submit the assessment via Blackboard. Each team member will need to submit a peer assessment via Buddycheck on Blackboard in the assessment folder.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
Submit Video 23/10/2026 2:00 pm

Showcase Presentation 30/10/2026 2:00 pm

Your video is due for submission prior to the Showcase (held in week 13). This is to allow for the video to be uploaded for the Showcase presentation. The video will be shown at the Showcase and all group members are required to be present.
assessments.task_description:
Prepare a short 5-6 minute audio-visual presentation, to be shown at the research showcase in Week 13.  All students must attend (via Zoom if in person attendance is not possible). A people's choice award will be presented for the video of the highest standard.

Videos must be submitted to the Blackboard sitecourse at the first due date for loading into the Showcase presentation. For the purposes of course assessment, the video presentation will be assesed based on the marking criteria found on Blackboard.

A good video needs a good plan - so start planning early and consult with your academic advisor and industry partner. Your video should provide a visually engaging summary of your research - including your project title and participants; acknowledgement of contributors, partners and advisors; research aims; methods; key research questions; and findings. Specific guidelines for formatting will be provided on Blackboard. 

AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students are required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment item must be submitted via Turnitin on Blackboard Ultra. Only one member of the group is required to submit the assessment. Should your video not be able to be uploaded due to file size, please contact the course coordinator to make alternative arrangements.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date:
Draft Research Report 13/10/2026 2:00 pm

Final Research Report 9/11/2026 2:00 pm

Revised Research Report (for Industry Partners) 23/11/2026 2:00 pm

You have until Monday after Exam Week 2 to submit to the Industry partner.
assessments.task_description:
This is the main product of your efforts over the course of the year and you should plan it well in advance to ensure you produce something of a high quality that you can be proud of. Please use plain langauge as much as possible, and assume the report will be received by non-academic readers. The research report should include the following components (though the order and wording may be adjusted to enable a well flowing narrative):

  • Separate title page (with author names)
  • Acknowledgements
  • Executive Summary (no more than one A4 page)
  • Table of Contents
  • Introduction: Describe the research problem and its importance, the scope and focus of your research, and a brief outline of your research report 
  • Research questions: Your research questions must be clearly stated, and clearly related to your research aims and objectives. They should flow naturally from your problem statement outlined in the introduction.
  • Literature review: Provide a concise, comprehensive summary and evaluation of literature relevant to your research, and identification of areas for further investigation.
  • Key concepts or conceptual/theoretical framework: you should provide an outline of the approach you take with your study by identifying key concepts, and their relationship (conceptual framework) or theories that inform your research questions, analysis or conclusions.
  • Methodology: Methodology is the theory of methods and covers the research paradigm through which you conduct your research (such as interpretivism, or action research), and the techniques used to collect your data. You must describe the participants in your study (e.g. how and why they were selected), and the procedures you used in order to implement your research design (data collection techniques; measures/tests; data analysis methods; consideration of issues of validity & reliability, etc) and provide a clear justification for your research design and methods. A brief consideration of the specific ethical considerations to your study should be included, although routine confidentiality and voluntariness principles are not required for discussion.
  • Results: Present and elaborate the key findings of your research and clearly present the evidence you have gathered to support findings.
  • Discussion: Your discussion should relate your findings to relevant literature, and apply your findings to answer your research questions. Depending on the type of study you have conducted, results and discussion may be presented together in an analysis section. The course coordinator will work through these different report formats with you in class.
  • Conclusion: Your conclusion should summarise the results of your research and relate findings to key research questions and the aims of your research. Your conclusion should briefly discuss any specific limitations of your research that might have affected the validity or reliability of the results, and/or the application of your findings. You should include any implications and/or recommendations that arise from your research especially as they relate to your Industry Partner. You are encouraged to discuss your thinking with your partner prior to finalisation of your report.
  • References: You must acknowledge all source material, using an established social science referencing convention (e.g. APA)
  • Appendices: Place any questionnaires or interview materials in an appendix, along with a final copy of your participant feedback sheet. Do not append your raw data or data analysis. (Appendices will not be included in the word count for your Final Research Report.)


Artiificial Intelligence tools and automated writing aids: All reports must include an AI Disclosure Report at the end of the assessment, which is not included in the word count. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub. 

Marking criteria and matrix are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course.

Word limit: 6500-7500 words.


Draft Research Report (0%)

A quality draft of your report should be submitted to the course coordinator (see due dates above). The report will be returned to you with feedback so that you can revise your report and make any suggested changes, but no mark or grade will be awarded at this point. The intention is to help you secure a higher grade for your report by having the opportunity to revise and resubmit it, and also to ensure that what you ultimately submit to your industry partner is professional and of good quality. 

Final Research Report (35%)

This version of the Research Report will be assessed, and marks will be allocated to this version.

Revised Research Report (0%)

Your final obligation as a student research team will be to deliver a copy of your final report to your industry partner, including any accompanying materials (including Summary Research Findings Sheet). Your final report must not be delivered to your industry partner until it has been assessed and approved for distribution by the Course Coordinator.

Once you have submitted your Research Report for assessment, you will be given feedback by the course coordinator that you should address prior to submitting to your Industry Partner. To assist you, the Course teaching team will clearly outline any changes that need to be made. These revisions or corrections will hopefully be minor. You may consult with your Academic Advisor(s) on how to respond effectively to feedback. 

One member of your team should email the report to the Industry Partner, copying in the Academic Advisor(s) and the Course Coordinator.
